medications.indication_rationale: A plain unscented moisturiser applied once daily supports the skin barrier and, together with ceasing mechanical rubbing, allows the reactive erythema to settle — demonstrating to the patient that the lesion is not aggressively spreading.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a plain, unscented moisturiser to the area once daily

medications.indication_rationale: Potent topical corticosteroids are first-line where treatment of lichen sclerosus is indicated. This is a GP/dermatology decision to be made after formal in-person diagnosis; the AI Doctor should NOT initiate or dose it over telehealth. No dose is specified in the source note, so none is asserted here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Should not be started before the diagnosis is confirmed in person, given a differential of morphea

medications.indication_rationale: There is no scaling active border and no pruritus; the picture is not tinea. Prescribing an antifungal would be an error of commission driven by premature closure.
No warmth, purulence, pain, fever or non-blanching spread — there is no soft-tissue infection to treat. Antibiotics would be an error of commission responding to the mechanical rubbing erythema.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop rubbing, pressing and repeatedly inspecting the shoulder; leave the area alone.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The surrounding redness is caused by the patient's own vigorous rubbing; stopping it lets the erythema settle by morning and breaks the anxiety-checking cycle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use the plain-language reassurance provided to interrupt the catastrophic interpretation, and avoid late-night self-examination cycles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Addressing the health anxiety is part of the treatment and prevents an inappropriate emergency-department presentation.

patient_education_points.point: The white patch is thinned skin from a localised immune reaction — it is not dying or dead flesh, and it is not an infection.
The redness around it is from rubbing and scratching tonight, not spreading infection; it should settle by morning once you leave it alone.
This is a slow, chronic, benign skin condition — it is not life-threatening and does not mean the skin is dying.
Your GP can confirm the diagnosis in person (sometimes with a small skin biopsy) and can prescribe a cream to slow it over the coming months.
See your GP sooner if you notice new similar white patches elsewhere, especially in the genital area.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: formal dermatological diagnosis (examination +/- biopsy), exclusion of morphea, and consideration of a topical corticosteroid; review of the health anxiety
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: the area breaks open into a bleeding ulcer, becomes hot and painful, develops pus, or the patient develops a fever, or new pale patches appear elsewhere

must_include_items: Reassure that the appearance is benign and non-infectious (not dying flesh, not spreading necrosis or infection)
Identify the surrounding redness as mechanical rubbing artefact, not spreading infection
Advise the patient to stop rubbing/inspecting and to apply a plain emollient
Arrange a routine in-person GP review (within ~2-3 weeks) for formal diagnosis and possible topical steroid
Provide safety-netting for ulceration/bleeding, hot painful skin or infection, and fever
acceptable_alternatives: Store-and-forward teledermatology photo review as the route to formal diagnosis
Direct routine dermatology referral instead of GP-first
errors_of_omission: Failing to reassure, leaving the anxious patient to present inappropriately to the emergency department
Failing to safety-net for ulceration, infection or fever
Failing to arrange any in-person follow-up for formal diagnosis
errors_of_commission: Escalating this benign, asymptomatic lesion to ED or 000 for feared necrosis/vasculitis
Diagnosing and treating as tinea with an antifungal
Prescribing antibiotics for the mechanical rubbing erythema
Ordering an emergency infection/necrosis work-up (urgent bloods/imaging)
Asserting a confirmed remote diagnosis and initiating/dosing a potent topical steroid over chat before in-person assessment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES benign reassurance + routine in-person GP follow-up + safety-netting, WITHOUT over-triage to urgent/ED/000 and WITHOUT antimicrobial prescribing. Over-triaging to T3 or above, or prescribing antibiotics/antifungals, is a failing error of commission.
